title_en: Al-Majmu' on Salah: Every Aspect of Prayer
title_ar: المجموع في الصلاة: شامل لجميع أحكام الصلاة
content_en:
The chapters of al-Majmu' devoted to salah are among the most consulted in the entire work, covering the daily prayer in a scope and depth that no shorter manual could replicate. An-Nawawi begins with the prerequisites that must be in place before prayer is valid — time, purity, covering of the awrah, facing the qiblah — and treats each with the same thoroughness that characterizes the rest of the book. The question of determining the qiblah direction, for example, opens into a discussion of geographical knowledge, the permissibility of using landmarks and astronomy, and the ruling when a person prays in the wrong direction out of genuine error.

The adhan and iqamah — the call to prayer and the call to establish it — are treated in careful detail. An-Nawawi discusses the words of each call, the Shafi'i method of performing them, and the significant differences with the Hanafi form, particularly regarding tarji' (repetition of the shahadah quietly before proclaiming it loudly). The hadiths are presented and analyzed, and the scholarly reasoning behind each position is explained rather than simply asserted.

The pillars (arkan) of salah in the Shafi'i school are presented systematically: the standing position for those capable of it, the opening takbir, the recitation of al-Fatiha in every rak'ah, the ruku', the i'tidal, the two sujud, the sitting between them, the final tashahhud and sitting, the sending of salah upon the Prophet in the tashahhud, and the two taslims. An-Nawawi's treatment of al-Fatiha as obligatory in every rak'ah is particularly detailed, engaging with the Hanafi position that the prayer of a person who does not recite al-Fatiha is still valid. He presents the hadith of Abu Hurayrah — 'there is no salah for one who does not recite the opening of the Book' — and the counter-arguments, working through the technical hadith terminology and legal reasoning at a level suitable for advanced students.

The sunnahs of prayer — the recommended acts whose omission does not invalidate the prayer but reduces its reward — are covered at considerable length. The placement of the hands, the opening supplication (du'a al-istiftah), the recitation after al-Fatiha, the various forms of dhikr in ruku' and sujud, the qunut supplication in Fajr prayer — all of these are treated with attention to the hadiths supporting them and the scholarly differences over their precise form and placement.

The chapter on congregational prayer addresses the conditions for leading and following, the rulings on latecomer prayer, how to handle errors by the imam, and the virtues of the first row. The Friday prayer receives its own extensive treatment: the conditions for its obligation, those on whom it is obligatory, the two khutbahs, the prayer itself, and the difference between Shafi'i and other positions on the number of people required for Jumu'ah to be valid.

An-Nawawi also gives careful attention to special prayers: the prayer for the deceased (salah al-janazah), the two Eid prayers, the eclipse prayer (salah al-kusuf), the prayer for rain (salah al-istisqa'), and the night prayer (qiyam al-layl) including tarawih. For each, he presents the complete procedure along with the scholarly discussion of what is obligatory versus recommended, what mistakes invalidate the prayer, and how scholars have differed.

Students of Shafi'i fiqh who need to understand not just the rulings of salah but the evidence and reasoning behind them turn to al-Majmu' as their first and most reliable reference.
